title: Swap and Duplicate Lines
subtitle: {key:d}{key:d}{key:p} swaps two lines. {key:y}{key:y}{key:p} duplicates one.
summary: Two of the oldest Vim recipes: {key:d}{key:d}{key:p} moves the current line down past the next one (a swap); {key:y}{key:y}{key:p} duplicates the current line. Both compose primitives you already know.

blocks.text: Two recipes you'll see in every "Vim tricks" list. They aren't *features* — they're compositions of primitives that happen to do something useful.

rows: Swap current line with the one below
Swap current line with the one above

blocks.title: Why It Works
blocks.text:
{key:d}{key:d} cuts the current line into the unnamed register. The cursor lands on what was the *next* line. {key:p} pastes the line back — but {key:p} pastes a *line-wise* register **below** the cursor's line, putting the cut line one position later than where it started. Net effect: swap.

{key:y}{key:y} yanks the line; the cursor doesn't move. {key:p} pastes below. Net effect: duplicate.

{key:x}{key:p}: {key:x} deletes the character under the cursor (into unnamed reg) and the cursor moves to what was the *next* char. {key:p} pastes a character-wise register **after** the cursor — placing the cut character one position later. Swap.
Move multiple lines? `:m` is cleaner: `:m+1` moves current line down one. Visual + `:m` moves a block.

blocks.title: Recipes work because everything shares the type tag
blocks.text: {key:x}{key:p}, {key:d}{key:d}{key:p}, {key:y}{key:y}{key:p} are not magic tricks — they're proofs that the register type tag (charwise vs linewise) and the type-aware paste algorithm are coherent. {key:y}{key:y} stores `(line_text, line)`, then {key:p} reads the `line` tag and pastes on a new line below. The whole composition is a one-line regression test for the register/paste contract.
